text: The surgeon uses a tips-up grasper to place a purse string suture above the expected fluid level in the colon, preventing spillage. Four robotic arms are docked to facilitate the procedure. An enterotomy is created using curved monopolar scissors, with a suction irrigator immediately available in the fourth arm.

With the colon decompressed, the surgeon identifies the anatomy, including the transverse colon and appendix, distal to the volvulus. Landmarks proximal and distal to the volvulus are established, and the surgeon begins transecting the mesocolon, ensuring the transection is close to the intestine to preserve perfusion and minimize bleeding risk.

The surgeon rotates the bowel to ensure complete transection of the mesentery from the involved intestine using a vessel sealer, enhancing exposure of the surgical field. After injecting ICG, the fluorescent camera is used to evaluate perfusion at the proximal and distal staple lines, confirming adequate blood flow to the bowel.

The surgeon removes the disconnected pathologic bowel from the surgical field and proceeds with an ileocolic anastomosis. Hemorrhagic ascites are aspirated before the anastomosis to prevent exposure to pressurized bowel contents. Enterotomies are created with stay sutures held upright to prevent passive flow of enteric contents into the abdomen. The anastomosis is completed with a two-layer closure.
The surgeon uses a 2-0 Vicryl suture to close the mucosal layer and a 3-0 PDS suture to imbricate the suture line during the anastomosis.
